selected_important_turn.text: All the food is too much, I would need at least one package of the food for my group. I was thinking you could have 2 of the food packages and I have 1, then I get all the water and the majority of the firewood. That leaves you with 2 of the high priority food packages and a package of firewood to cook the food.
selected_important_turn.why_important: This is the first concrete counteroffer that shaped the division of scarce items (food, firewood, water). It set the main trade-off (food vs. water/firewood) and therefore strongly influenced subsequent reactions and the final outcome.
selected_important_turn.why_improvable_or_worth_amplifying: Although cooperative in intent, it ignored a concrete worry the opponent would raise (that one package of firewood may not be enough to cook two food packages). The offer emphasizes the speaker's retention of water and most firewood without explicitly addressing the opponent's cooking need, which made the opponent respond that the split is "quite unfair." The move was consequential but could have better prevented escalation and created more joint value.
selected_important_turn.better_direction: Before or while proposing the split, explicitly acknowledge the opponent's cooking constraint and either (a) ask how much firewood they need to cook two packages, or (b) offer a compensating adjustment (e.g., an extra firewood package, or a water-for-firewood trade) or propose a contingency (share firewood or swap some water later). Focus the offer on meeting both parties' critical needs rather than only asserting retained items.

expected_effect_on_negotiation.joint_gain: increase — by surfacing the opponent's real constraint, you enable package deals that satisfy both needs rather than locking into a zero-sum split.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.fairness: increase — explicitly addressing the cooking need makes the offer feel more balanced and attentive to the other's priorities.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.ease_of_agreement: increase — reduces likelihood of protest/reluctant rejection and speeds convergence by removing a clear objection.
expected_effect_on_negotiation.relationship: improve — shows empathy and reduces perceived selfishness, building trust.

value2reason.Low: I need water to help cook and drink.
value2reason.Medium: I need extra firewood to cook the food.
value2reason.High: I am staying longer and need more food for everyone.

value2reason.Low: I have children in my group and they wanted to make smores but we would need extra firewood to do so.
value2reason.Medium: One person in my group has an issue and dehydrates easily.
value2reason.High: I have more people that require more food.

dialogue.text: I am doing quite good. I enjoy camping. I however did not bring enough food.
dialogue.speaker: mturk_agent_1

dialogue.text: Ok I'm sure we can work it out because I know I need extra water and firewood. Do you have any ideas on how to divvy up the extra supplies?
dialogue.speaker: mturk_agent_2

dialogue.text: I would need some firewood as well, as I need it to cook the food. I was thinking maybe you can have all the water and I have all the food.
dialogue.speaker: mturk_agent_1

dialogue.text: All the food is too much, I would need at least one package of the food for my group. I was thinking you could have 2 of the food packages and I have 1, then I get all the water and the majority of the firewood. That leaves you with 2 of the high priority food packages and a package of firewood to cook the food.
dialogue.speaker: mturk_agent_2

dialogue.text: I think that could work. Let me confirm: you take 1 food, all water, and 2 firewood; I take 2 food and 1 firewood. That covers all packages, right?
dialogue.speaker: mturk_agent_1

dialogue.text: I see your point. Let me adjust to ensure we both have enough for cooking. How about I give you 1 food, 2 water, and 2 firewood, while I take 2 food, 1 water, and 1 firewood? This way, we both have enough for cooking and I still get more of my high-priority items.
dialogue.speaker: mturk_agent_2

dialogue.text: I think that's a fair split. Let me confirm: you get 2 food, 1 water, and 1 firewood; I get 1 food, 2 water, and 2 firewood. That covers all packages and meets our needs.
dialogue.speaker: mturk_agent_1

dialogue.text: I see your point. To ensure we both have enough for cooking, how about I give you 1 food, 2 water, and 2 firewood, while I take 2 food, 1 water, and 1 firewood? This way, we both have enough for cooking and I still get more of my high-priority items.
dialogue.speaker: mturk_agent_2

dialogue.text: I think that's a fair split. Let me confirm: you get 2 food, 1 water, and 1 firewood; I get 1 food, 2 water, and 2 firewood. That covers all packages and meets our needs.
dialogue.speaker: mturk_agent_1

dialogue.text: I think we're close. Let me adjust to make sure we both have enough for cooking. How about I give you 1 food, 1 water, and 2 firewood, while I take 2 food, 2 water, and 1 firewood? This way, we both have enough for cooking and I still get more of my high-priority items.
dialogue.speaker: mturk_agent_2

dialogue.text: I think we've found a good balance. Let me confirm: you get 2 food, 2 water, and 1 firewood; I get 1 food, 1 water, and 2 firewood. That covers all packages and ensures we both have what we need for cooking.
dialogue.speaker: mturk_agent_1

dialogue.text: I think we've reached a fair split. Final allocation: I get 2 food, 2 water, and 1 firewood; you get 1 food, 1 water, and 2 firewood. All packages are covered, and we both have what we need for cooking.
dialogue.speaker: mturk_agent_2

dialogue.text: The deal has already been confirmed. I am leaving now.
dialogue.speaker: mturk_agent_1
